attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of vomiting and profuse watery diarrhoea occurring up to 12 times per day. She has been unable to keep down any food or fluids. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She recently returned from a holiday in Southeast Asia.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. She has dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor. Her chest is clear and heart sounds are normal.

Laboratory investigations reveal:

Sodium: 135 mmol/L (135-145)
Potassium: 3.5 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
Phosphate: 0.5 mmol/L (0.8-1.5)
Creatinine: 210 μmol/L (45-90)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate phosphate replacement before fluid resuscitation
Urgent haemodialysis for acute kidney injury
Empirical antibiotic therapy with supportive care
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe dehydration secondary to gastroenteritis, evidenced by clinical signs of volume depletion (tachycardia, hypotension, dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor) and acute kidney injury from prerenal causes. The immediate priority is fluid resuscitation to restore circulating volume and prevent circulatory collapse.

Intravenous fluid resuscitation is the most appropriate initial management. The patient requires immediate restoration of intravascular volume with crystalloid solutions, followed by ongoing monitoring and correction of electrolyte abnormalities including the hypophosphataemia as clinically indicated.

Oral rehydration solution with electrolyte supplements is contraindicated in this patient who has persistent vomiting and cannot tolerate oral intake. Oral rehydration therapy is only appropriate when patients can maintain oral intake.

Immediate phosphate replacement before fluid resuscitation prioritises the wrong intervention. While hypophosphataemia needs addressing, the immediate threat to life is circulatory compromise from severe dehydration, which must be corrected first. At present, the hypophosphataemia is mild-moderate and not clearly symptomatic. It is likely due to gastrointestinal losses from the diarrhoea, and should resolve once the diarrhoea settles and oral intake resumes. Oral replacement is unlikely to be needed. Intravenous phosphate replacement is usually reserved for severe cases where phosphate levels are <0.32 mmol/L. Severe hypophosphataemia can lead to serious complications including muscle weakness, respiratory failure, cardiac dysfunction, and altered mental status.

Urgent haemodialysis for acute kidney injury is not indicated as this appears to be prerenal acute kidney injury secondary to volume depletion. Creatinine levels alone are not indicative of acute dialysis requirements, and creatinine elevation should improve with adequate fluid resuscitation. Indications for emergency dialysis include severe, medically-refractory acidosis; severe, medically-refractory electrolyte disturbances like hyperkalaemia; intoxication with certain substances; severe, medically-refractory fluid overload; and severe uraemic symptoms.

Empirical antibiotic therapy with supportive care may be considered later depending on clinical course and stool culture results, but does not address the immediate life-threatening dehydration. Most gastroenteritis cases are viral and self-limiting, making empirical antibiotics inappropriate as initial management.

The comprehensive management plan includes fluid resuscitation, electrolyte monitoring and replacement, investigation of the underlying cause, and symptomatic support. However, restoration of circulating volume takes priority over specific electrolyte correction in this critically dehydrated patient.
learningPoints: Severe diarrhoea with vomiting requires immediate intravenous fluid resuscitation to correct dehydration and prevent circulatory collapse.
Electrolyte abnormalities including hypophosphataemia should be monitored and corrected as part of comprehensive fluid and electrolyte management.
Oral rehydration is contraindicated when patients cannot tolerate oral intake due to persistent vomiting.
references: Sharma S, Hashmi MF, Castro D. Hypophosphatemia. In: StatPearls [online]. 2022. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493172/

Stubbs JR. Hypophosphatemia: Evaluation and treatment [Internet]. UpToDate. 2025 [cited 2025 May 25]. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/hypophosphatemia-evaluation-and-treatment?search=hypophosphatemia&source=search_result&selectedTitle=1~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=1

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
